actions.action_label: Talladega College is accredited by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges
actions.notes: Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges (SACSCOC) http://www.sacscoc.org Disclosure Statement Regarding the Status of TALLADEGA COLLEGE Talladega, Alabama Issued June 26, 2025, by SACSCOC The following publicly available information is provided by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges (SACSCOC) concerning the accreditation of Talladega College. The information presented below is in accordance with SACSCOC’s disclosure policy; SACSCOC staff cannot comment further on questions specifically related to Talladega College. The institution reviewed this statement prior to public posting. Action by SACSCOC Board of Trustees took place on June 12, 2025, and the institution’s next review is June 2026. What is the accreditation status of Institution? Talladega College is accredited by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges. The institution was placed on Warning following the Board’s review of a Special Report following receipt of unsolicited information concerning compliance with Core Requirement 13.1 (Financial responsibility), Core Requirement 13.2 (Financial documents), Standard 13.3 (Financial responsibility), and Standard 13.5 (Control of sponsored research/external funds). SACSCOC accreditation includes all components of the institution—all programs, branch campuses, off-campus sites and distance learning programs as reported to SACSCOC; thus, the Warning status applies to the entire institution. What does it mean to be continued in accreditation and placed on Warning? Warning imposed by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees follows a determination of significant non-compliance with the Core Requirements or Standards of the Principles of Accreditation, the accreditation standards of SACSCOC; failure to make timely and significant progress toward correcting the deficiencies that led to the findings of non-compliance; or failure to comply with SACSCOC policies and procedures. An institution placed or continued on Warning remains in accredited status while on sanction. The maximum total time during a monitoring period that an institution may be on Warning is two years. In June 2026, Talladega College will have been on Warning for twelve (12) months. For additional information about monitoring periods and sanctions, see the SACSCOC policy entitled Sanctions, Denial of Reaffirmation, and Removal from Membership. Why was Talladega College placed on Warning? Talladega College was continued in accreditation and placed on Warning because the SACSCOC Board of Trustees determined that the institution had failed to demonstrate compliance with Core Requirements 13.1 (Financial resources), 13.2 (Financial documents), Standard 13.3 (Financial responsibility), Standard 13.5 (Control of sponsored research/external funds) of the Principles of Accreditation. actions.notes: Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges (SACSCOC) http://www.sacscoc.org Disclosure Statement Regarding the Status of FLORIDA MEMORIAL UNIVERSITY Miami Gardens, FL Issued January 8, 2026, by SACSCOC The following publicly available information is provided by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges (SACSCOC) concerning the accreditation of Florida Memorial University. The information presented below is in accordance with the SACSCOC disclosure policy; SACSCOC staff cannot comment further on questions specifically related to Florida Memorial University. The institution reviewed this statement prior to public posting and has 60 days to provide an official comment to be added to this disclosure regarding the action taken by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees. On December 7, 2025, the SACSCOC Board of Trustees continued the accreditation of Florida Memorial University for Good Cause and placed the institution on Probation for twelve months. The institution’s next review will occur in December 2026. What is the accreditation status of Florida Memorial University? Florida Memorial University remains an accredited member of the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges while on the sanction of Probation for Good Cause. Because SACSCOC is an institutional accreditor, this status applies to all components of the institution—all programs, branch campuses, off- campus sites, and distance education programs as reported to SACSCOC. What does it mean to be continued in accreditation, and placed on Probation for Good Cause? If an institution has not demonstrated compliance with all the standards of the Principles of Accreditation at the conclusion of its two-year maximum monitoring period, the SACSCOC Board of Trustees must (1) remove the institution from membership or (2) continue accreditation for Good Cause. If accreditation is extended for Good Cause, the institution must also be placed on Probation for Good Cause. Florida Memorial University was at the end of its two-year monitoring period in December 2025, however, instead of removing the institution’s accreditation, the SACSCOC Board of Trustees extended the institution’s accreditation because it met all three of the provisions for Good Cause, which include (1) the institution has demonstrated significant recent accomplishments in addressing non- compliance, and (2) the institution has provided evidence which makes it reasonable for the Board to assume it will remedy all deficiencies within a 12–month period, and (3) the institution has provided assurance to the Board that it is not aware of any other reasons, other than those identified by the Board, why the institution cannot be continued for Good Cause. Probation is the most serious public sanction imposed by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees short of loss of accreditation. In December 2026, Florida Memorial University will have been on Probation for Good Cause for one (1) year. For additional information about sanctions, see the SACSCOC policy entitled Sanctions, Denial of Reaffirmation and Removal from Membership. The SACSCOC Board of Trustees determined that Florida Memorial University has not yet demonstrated compliance with the following standards of the Principles of Accreditation: Standard 13.3 (Financial responsibility), This standard expects an institution to manage its financial resources in a responsible manner. The information presented below is in accordance with the SACSCOC disclosure policy; SACSCOC staff cannot comment further on questions specifically related to Union Commonwealth University. The institution reviewed this statement prior to public posting and has 60 days to provide an official comment to be added to this disclosure regarding the action taken by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees. On December 7, 2025, the SACSCOC Board of Trustees denied reaffirmation, continued accreditation, and placed Union Commonwealth University on Warning for twelve months. The institution’s next review will occur in December 2026. What is the accreditation status of Union Commonwealth University? Union Commonwealth University remains an accredited member of the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges while on the sanction of Warning. Because SACSCOC is an institutional accreditor, this status applies to the all components of the institution—all programs, branch campuses, off-campus sites, and distance education programs as reported to SACSCOC. What does it mean to be continued in accreditation and placed on Warning? The sanction of Warning is imposed by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees in the early stages of institutional review following a determination of non-compliance with one or more of the Core Requirements and/or Standards of the Principles of Accreditation, the accreditation standards of SACSCOC; failure to make timely and significant progress toward correcting the deficiencies that led to the findings of non-compliance; or failure to comply with SACSCOC policies and procedures. The maximum total time during a monitoring period that an institution may be on sanction is two (2) years. In December 2026, Union Commonwealth University will have been on Warning for one (1) year. For additional information about sanctions, see the SACSCOC policy entitled Sanctions, Denial of Reaffirmation, and Removal from Membership. Why was Union Commonwealth University denied reaffirmation and placed on Warning? Union Commonwealth University was denied reaffirmation of accreditation, placed on Warning, and authorized to receive a Special Committee visit because the SACSCOC Board of Trustees determined that the institution has not yet demonstrated compliance with the following standards of the Principles of Accreditation: Core Requirement 6.1 (Full-time faculty) This standard expects an institution to employ an adequate number of full-time faculty members to support the mission and goals of the institution. Core Requirement 7.1 (Institutional planning) This standard expects an institution to engage in ongoing, comprehensive, and integrated research-based planning and evaluation processes that (a) focus on institutional quality and effectiveness and (b) incorporate a systematic review of institutional goals and outcomes consistent with its mission. Core Requirement 13.1 (Financial resources) This standard expects an institution to have sound financial resources and a demonstrated, stable financial base to support the mission of the institution and the scope of its programs and services. What is the accreditation status of Institution? Johnson C. Smith University is accredited by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges. The institution has been continued in accreditation for Good Cause and placed on Probation for twelve months after the Board’s review of its Second Monitoring Report following action on a Follow-Up Report that was originally reviewed in June 2023. SACSCOC accreditation includes all components of the institution—all programs, branch campuses, off-campus sites and distance learning programs as reported to SACSCOC; thus, the Probation for Good Cause status applies to the entire institution. What does it mean to be continued in accreditation and placed on Probation for Good Cause? Johnson C. Smith University had been monitored for two (2) years and the maximum consecutive time for a monitoring period is two (2) years. Johnson C. Smith University reached the end of its two-year monitoring period. Instead of removing the institution’s accreditation, the SACSCOC Board of Trustees can act to extend accreditation for Good Cause if (1) the institution has demonstrated significant recent accomplishments in addressing non- compliance, and (2) the institution has provided evidence which makes it reasonable for the Board to assume it will remedy all deficiencies within a 12–month period, and (3) the institution has provided assurance to the Board that it is not aware of any other reasons, other than those identified by the Board, why the institution cannot be continued for Good Cause. Probation for Good Cause is the most serious public sanction imposed by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees short of loss of accreditation. In June 2026, Johnson C. Smith University will have been on Probation for Good Cause for one year. For additional information about monitoring periods and sanctions, see the SACSCOC policy entitled Sanctions, Denial of Reaffirmation, and Removal from Membership. Why was Johnson C. The institution has reviewed this statement prior to public posting. Action by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees took place on June 12, 2025, and the institution’s next review is June 2026. What is the accreditation status of the institution? Pfeiffer University is accredited by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges. The institution was placed on Warning following the Board’s review of a Special Report submitted following receipt of unsolicited information from the U.S. Department of Education indicating the institution's federal composite score failed to meet the standards for financial responsibility for fiscal year ended June 30, 2023. SACSCOC accreditation includes all components of the institution—all programs, branch campuses, off-campus sites and distance learning programs as reported to SACSCOC; thus, the Warning status applies to the entire institution. What does it mean to be continued in accreditation and placed on Warning? Warning imposed by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees follows a determination of significant non-compliance with the Core Requirements or Standards of the Principles of Accreditation, the accreditation standards of SACSCOC; failure to make timely and significant progress toward correcting the deficiencies that led to the findings of non-compliance; or failure to comply with SACSCOC policies and procedures. An institution placed or continued on Warning remains in accredited status while on sanction. The maximum total time during a monitoring period that an institution may be on Warning is two years. In June 2026, Pfeiffer University will have been on Warning for one year. For additional information about sanctions, see the SACSCOC policy entitled Sanctions, Denial of Reaffirmation, and Removal from Membership. Why was Pfeiffer University placed on Warning? Pfeiffer University was placed on Warning because the SACSCOC Board of Trustees determined that the institution had failed to demonstrate compliance with Core Requirement 13.1 (Financial resources) and Standard 13.3 (Financial responsibility) of the Principles of Accreditation. To read the full statements for the standards cited above, access the Principles of Accreditation: Foundations for Quality Enhancement. What will happen in June 2026? The SACSCOC Board of Trustees will consider the accreditation status of Pfeiffer University following a review of the requested Monitoring Report addressing the standards cited above for non-compliance. The Board will have the following options: (1) remove the institution from Warning without an additional report; (2) remove the institution from Warning and request a Follow-Up Report at the time of the submission of the Fifth-Year Interim Report; (3) continue the institution on Warning and request a Second Monitoring Report, with or without a Special Committee authorized, or (4) remove the institution from membership for failure to comply with the Principles of Accreditation. actions.notes: August 11, 2025 Doug Wade Interim President Warner Pacific University 2219 S.E. 68th Avenue Portland, OR 97215 Dear President Wade: This letter serves as formal notification and official record of action taken by the Northwest Commission on Colleges and Universities (NWCCU) at its meeting on July 8-11, 2025, concerning the Spring 2025 Financial Resources Review and the Spring 2025 Ad Hoc Report of Warner Pacific University. Accreditation Accept the Reports, Continue Sanction of Warning Status of Previous Recommendations Addressed in this Evaluation • Recommendation 1: Spring 2020 Evaluation of Institutional Effectiveness - Fulfilled • Recommendation 2: Spring 2020 Evaluation of Institutional Effectiveness - Continued as Non-Compliant Recommendations Out of Compliance The Commission finds that the following Recommendations are areas where Warner Pacific University is out of compliance with the NWCCU Standards for Accreditation. According to U.S. Department of Education Regulation 34 CFR 602.20 and Commission Policy, the Commission requires that Warner Pacific University take appropriate action to ensure these Recommendations are addressed and resolved within the prescribed four-year period, by no later than July 2028. The Commission recommends that Warner Pacific University: • Recommendation 2: Spring 2020 Evaluation of Institutional Effectiveness - Demonstrate short and long- term financial health and stability by strategically growing enrollment, controlling expenses, and becoming less dependent on unrestricted giving to cover operating deficits. (2020 Standard(s) 2.E.1;2.E.2) Future Evaluations • Year 6 - Standard 2 - Policies, Regulations, and Financial Review Spring 2026 • Financial Resources Review Spring 2026 o Recommendation 2: Spring 2020 Evaluation of Institutional Effectiveness • Year 7 - Evaluation of Institutional Effectiveness Spring 2027 NWCCU is committed to an accreditation process that adds value to institutions while contributing to public accountability, and we thank you for your continued support of this process. If you have questions about any of the information in this letter, please contact your staff liaison, Dr. Gita Bangera, at gbangera@nwccu.org. Sincerely, Selena M. Grace President cc: Ms. Elizabeth Wilson, Director of Institutional Effectiveness: IR & Accreditation Mr. Mike Moreland, Chairman of the Board Mr. Ben Cannon, Executive Director, Oregon Higher Education Coordinating Commission 8060 165th Ave NE, Suite 200 | Redmond, WA 98052 | nwccu.org

actions.notes: Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges (SACSCOC) http://www.sacscoc.org Disclosure Statement Regarding the Status of KING UNIVERSITY Bristol, Tennessee Issued June 26, 2025, by SACSCOC The following publicly available information is provided by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges (SACSCOC) concerning the accreditation of King University. Information presented below is in accord with SACSCOC’s disclosure policy; SACSCOC staff cannot comment further on questions specifically related to King University. The institution has reviewed this statement prior to public posting. Action by SACSCOC Board of Trustees took place on June 12, 2025, and the institution’s next review is June 2026. What is the accreditation status of King University? King University is accredited by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges. The institution was continued on Warning following the Board’s review of a Monitoring Report following submission of a Follow-Up Report in June 2024. SACSCOC accreditation includes all components of the institution—all programs, branch campuses, off-campus sites, and distance learning programs as reported to SACSCOC; thus, the Warning status applies to the entire institution. What does it mean to be continued in accreditation and continued on Warning? Warning imposed by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees follows a determination of significant non-compliance with the Core Requirements or Standards of the Principles of Accreditation, the accreditation standards of SACSCOC; failure to make timely and significant progress toward correcting the deficiencies that led to the findings of non-compliance; or failure to comply with SACSCOC policies and procedures. An institution placed or continued on Warning remains in accredited status while on sanction. The maximum total time during a monitoring period that an institution may be on Warning is two years. In June 2026, King University will have been on Warning for two years and, therefore, will have to be found in compliance on the standards for which it is being monitored or meet the provisions of Good Cause to maintain its accreditation. For additional information about monitoring periods and sanctions, see the SACSCOC policy entitled “Sanctions, Denial of Reaffirmation, and Removal from Membership.” Why was King University placed on Warning? King University was continued in accreditation and continued on Warning because the SACSCOC Board of Trustees determined that the institution has not yet demonstrated compliance with Core Requirement 13.1 (Financial resources) and Standard 13.3 (Financial responsibilities). These standards expect an institution: (1) to have sound financial resources; and (2) to manage its financial resources and operate in a fiscally responsible manner. A Special Committee was not authorized to visit the institution. (To read the full statements for the standards cited above, access the Principles of Accreditation: Foundations for Quality Enhancement. What will happen in June 2026? The SACSCOC Board of Trustees will consider the accreditation status of King University following a review of a Second Monitoring Report submitted by the institution addressing the standards cited above for non-compliance. The Board will have the following options: (1) remove the institution from Warning without an additional report; (2) continue accreditation, place the institution on Probation for Good Cause, and request a Monitoring Report with a Special Committee authorized; or (3) remove the institution from membership for failure to comply with the Principles of Accreditation. The information presented below is in accordance with the SACSCOC disclosure policy; SACSCOC staff cannot comment further on questions specifically related to the University of Lynchburg. The institution reviewed this statement prior to public posting and has 60 days to provide an official comment to be added to this disclosure regarding the action taken by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees. On December 7, 2025, the SACSCOC Board of Trustees denied reaffirmation, continued accreditation, and continued the University of Lynchburg on Warning for twelve months. The institution’s next review will occur in December 2026. What is the accreditation status of the University of Lynchburg? The University of Lynchburg remains an accredited member of SACSCOC while denied reaffirmation and on the sanction of Warning. Because SACSCOC is an institutional accreditor, this status applies to the all components of the institution—all programs, branch campuses, off-campus sites, and distance education programs as reported to SACSCOC. What does it mean to be continued in accreditation and continued on Warning? The sanction of Warning is imposed by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees in the early stages of institutional following a determination of non-compliance with one or more of the Core Requirements and/or Standards of the Principles of Accreditation, the accreditation standards of SACSCOC; failure to make timely and significant progress toward correcting the deficiencies that led to the findings of non-compliance; or failure to comply with SACSCOC policies and procedures. In December 2026, the University of Lynchburg will have been on Warning for two (2) years, which is the maximum total time allowed during one monitoring period. An institution’s accreditation can be extended into a second monitoring period for Good Cause. For additional information about sanctions, see the SACSCOC policy entitled Sanctions, Denial of Reaffirmation, and Removal from Membership. Why was the University of Lynchburg denied reaffirmation and continued on Warning? The University of Lynchburg was denied reaffirmation and continued on Warning because the SACSCOC Board of Trustees determined that the institution failed to demonstrate compliance with Standard 8.2.a (Student outcomes: educational outcomes) and Standard 13.3 (Financial responsibility) of the Principles of Accreditation. These standards expect the institution to identify expected outcomes, assess the extent to which it achieves these outcomes, and provide evidence of seeking improvement based on analysis of the results for each of its educational programs; and to manage its financial resources in a responsible manner. A Special Committee visit was not authorized. This determination follows a previous action taken by the Board in December 2024 which also indicated non-compliance with these standards, hence the continuation of the denial of reaffirmation and sanction of Warning. What will happen in December 2026? The SACSCOC Board of Trustees will consider the accreditation status of the University of Lynchburg following a review of a Second Monitoring Report submitted by the institution addressing the standards cited above. actions.action_label: The Southwestern Baptist Theological Seminary is accredited by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges
actions.notes: Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges (SACSCOC) http://www.sacscoc.org Disclosure Statement Regarding the Status of SOUTHWESTERN BAPTIST THEOLOGICAL SEMINARY Fort Worth, Texas Issued June 26, 2025, by SACSCOC The following publicly available information is provided by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges (SACSCOC) concerning the accreditation of The Southwestern Baptist Theological Seminary. The information presented below is in accord with SACSCOC’s disclosure policy; SACSCOC staff cannot comment further on questions specifically related to The Southwestern Baptist Theological Seminary. The institution reviewed this statement prior to public posting. Action by SACSCOC Board of Trustees took place on June 12, 2025, and the institution’s next review is June 2026. What is the accreditation status of Institution? The Southwestern Baptist Theological Seminary is accredited by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges. The institution has been continued in accreditation for Good Cause and placed on Probation for twelve months after the Board’s review of a Second Monitoring Report following submission of a Follow- Up Report addressing Core Requirement 13.1 (Financial resources) and Standard 13.3 (Financial responsibility). SACSCOC accreditation includes all components of the institution—all programs, branch campuses, off-campus sites and distance learning programs as reported to SACSCOC; thus, the Probation status applies to the entire institution. What does it mean to be continued in accreditation and placed on Probation for Good Cause]? The Southwestern Baptist Theological Seminary had been monitored for two (2) years and the maximum consecutive time for a monitoring period is two (2) years. The Southwestern Baptist Theological Seminary reached the end of its two-year monitoring period. Instead of removing the institution’s accreditation, the SACSCOC Board of Trustees can act to extend accreditation for Good Cause if (1) the institution has demonstrated significant recent accomplishments in addressing non-compliance, and (2) the institution has provided evidence which makes it reasonable for the Board to assume it will remedy all deficiencies within a 12–month period, and (3) the institution has provided assurance to the Board that it is not aware of any other reasons, other than those identified by the Board, why the institution cannot be continued for Good Cause. Probation for Good Cause is the most serious public sanction imposed by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees short of loss of accreditation. In June 2026, The Southwestern Baptist Theological Seminary will have been on Probation for Good Cause for one year. For additional information about monitoring periods and sanctions, see the SACSCOC policy entitled Sanctions, Denial of Reaffirmation, and Removal from Membership. Why was Southwestern Baptist Theological Seminary placed on Probation for Good Cause? Southwestern Baptist Theological Seminary was continued in accreditation, placed on Probation for Good Cause, and requested to submit a Third Monitoring Report because the SACSCOC Board of Trustees determined that the institution had failed to demonstrate compliance with Core Requirement 13.1 (Financial resources) and Standard 13.3 (Financial responsibility) of the Principles of Accreditation. These standards expect the institution to have sound financial resources and a demonstrated, stable financial base to support the mission of the institution and to manage its financial resources and operate in a fiscally responsible manner. Accreditation Reaffirm Accreditation: Remove the Sanction of Warning, Accept the Report Status of Previous Recommendations Addressed in this Evaluation • Recommendation 1: Fall 2021 Ad Hoc Report with Visit - Continued as Needs Improvement Recommendations Substantially in Compliance but in Need of Improvement The Commission recommends that Clatsop Community College: • Recommendation 1: Fall 2025 Ad Hoc Report with Visit - Systematically implement and continue to refine institutional planning processes to ensure that data collection and analysis support mission fulfillment and effective decision-making. actions.notes: Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges (SACSCOC) http://www.sacscoc.org Disclosure Statement Regarding the Status of Edgecombe Community College Tarboro, NC Issued January 8, 2026, by SACSCOC The following publicly available information is provided by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges (SACSCOC) concerning the accreditation of Edgecombe Community College. The information presented below is in accordance with the SACSCOC disclosure policy; SACSCOC staff cannot comment further on questions specifically related to Edgecombe Community College. The institution reviewed this statement prior to public posting and has 60 days to provide an official comment to be added to this disclosure regarding the action taken by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees. On December 7, 2025, the SACSCOC Board of Trustees placed Edgecombe Community College on Warning for six months. The institution’s next review will occur in June 2026. What is the accreditation status of Edgecombe Community College? Edgecombe Community College remains an accredited member of the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges while on the sanction of Warning. Because SACSCOC is an institutional accreditor, this status applies to all components of the institution—all programs, branch campuses, off- campus sites, and distance education programs as reported to SACSCOC. What does it mean to be placed on Warning? The sanction of Warning is imposed by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees in the early stages of institutional review following a determination of non-compliance with one or more of the Core Requirements and/or Standards of the Principles of Accreditation, the accreditation standards of SACSCOC; failure to make timely and significant progress toward correcting the deficiencies that led to the findings of non-compliance; or failure to comply with SACSCOC policies and procedures. The maximum total time during a monitoring period that an institution may be on sanction is two (2) years. In June 2026, Edgecombe Community College will be in its first year of monitoring. For additional information about sanctions, see the SACSCOC policy entitled Sanctions, Denial of Reaffirmation, and Removal from Membership. Why was Edgecombe Community College placed on Warning? Edgecombe Community College was placed on Warning because the SACSCOC Board of Trustees determined that the institution is in non-compliance with the Principles of Accreditation, namely, Core Requirement 8.1 (Student achievement), Standard 6.2.b (Program faculty), Standard 6.2.c (Program coordination), and Standard 8.2.a (Student outcomes: educational programs) following review of a Referral Report. A Special Committee was not authorized to visit the institution. These standards expect an institution to: (1) Identify, evaluate, and publish goals and outcomes for student achievement appropriate to the institution’s mission; (2) Employ a sufficient number of full-time faculty members to ensure curriculum and program quality, integrity, and review for each of its educational programs; (3) Assign appropriate responsibility for program coordination for each of its educational programs; and (4) Identify expected student learning outcomes, assess the extent to which it achieves these outcomes, and provide evidence of seeking improvement based on analysis of the results for each of its educational programs. Information presented below is in accord with SACSCOC’s disclosure policy; SACSCOC staff cannot comment further on questions specifically related to Galen College of Nursing. The institution has reviewed this statement prior to public posting. Action by SACSCOC Board of Trustees took place on June 12, 2025, and the institution’s next review is December 2025. What is the accreditation status of Galen College of Nursing? Galen College of Nursing is accredited by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges. The institution was placed on Warning following the Board’s review of a Referral Report subsequent to the institution’s Fifth-Year Interim Review in April 2024. SACSCOC accreditation includes all components of the institution—all programs, branch campuses, off-campus sites, and distance learning programs as reported to SACSCOC; thus, the Warning status applies to the entire institution. What does it mean to be continued in accreditation and placed on Warning? Warning imposed by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees follows a determination of significant non-compliance with the Core Requirements or Standards of the Principles of Accreditation, the accreditation standards of SACSCOC; failure to make timely and significant progress toward correcting the deficiencies that led to the findings of non-compliance; or failure to comply with SACSCOC policies and procedures. An institution placed or continued on Warning remains in accredited status while on sanction. The maximum total time during a monitoring period that an institution may be on Warning is two years. In December 2025, Galen College of Nursing will have been on Warning for six (6) months. For additional information about monitoring periods and sanctions, see the SACSCOC policy entitled Sanctions, Denial of Reaffirmation, and Removal from Membership. Why was Galen College of Nursing placed on Warning? Galen College of Nursing was continued in accreditation and placed on Warning because the SACSCOC Board of Trustees determined that the institution has not yet demonstrated compliance with Core Requirement 8.1 (Student achievement), Standard 8.2.a (Student outcomes: educational programs), and Standard 10.3 (Archived information) of the Principles of Accreditation. A Special Committee was not authorized to visit the institution. These standards expect an institution to (1) identify, evaluate, and publish goals and outcomes for student achievement appropriate to the institution’s mission, the nature of the students it serves, and the kinds of programs offered and, to use multiple measures to document student success; (2) to identify expected student learning outcomes, assess the extent to which it achieves these outcomes, and provide evidence of seeking improvement based on analysis of the results for each of its educational programs; and (3) to ensure the availability of archived official catalogs, digital or print, with relevant information for course and degree requirements sufficient to serve former and returning students. A Special Committee was not authorized to visit the institution. Accreditation Reaffirm Accreditation: Remove the Sanction of Probation, Accept the Report Status of Previous Recommendations Addressed in this Evaluation • Recommendation 1: Spring 2020 Mission Fulfillment and Sustainability - Continued as Needs Improvement • Recommendation 2: Spring 2020 Mission Fulfillment and Sustainability - Continued as Needs Improvement • Recommendation 2: Spring 2022 Special Report - Fulfilled • Recommendation 3: Spring 2022 Special Report - Fulfilled • Recommendation 7: Spring 2023 Special Report – Fulfilled • Recommendation 1: Fall 2024 Special Report - Fulfilled Recommendations Substantially in Compliance but in Need of Improvement The Commission recommends that North Idaho College: • Recommendation 1: Spring 2020 Mission Fulfillment and Sustainability - Establish meaningful indicators (metrics) to align with and measure institutional goals and objectives in its evaluation, planning, and resource allocation processes to support institutional effectiveness and student achievement. (2020 Standard(s) 1.B.1) • Recommendation 2: Spring 2020 Mission Fulfillment and Sustainability - Engage in an effective system of learning outcomes assessment processes across all academic and learning support programs and use the results of those assessments to evaluate the quality of learning and to inform academic and learning-support planning. (2020 Standard(s) 1.C.5; 1.C.7) Future Evaluations • Year 6 - Standard 2 - Policies, Regulations, and Financial Review Spring 2026 • Year 7 - Evaluation of Institutional Effectiveness Spring 2027 o Recommendation 1: Spring 2020 Mission Fulfillment and Sustainability o Recommendation 2: Spring 2020 Mission Fulfillment and Sustainability o Recommendation 2: Fall 2024 Special Report o Recommendation 3: Fall 2024 Special Report NWCCU is committed to an accreditation process that adds value to institutions while contributing to public accountability, and we commend North Idaho College for its meaningful progress and continued engagement in this process. If you have questions about any of the information in this letter, please contact your staff liaison, Dr. Ed Harri, at eharri@nwccu.org. Sincerely, Selena M. Grace President cc: Dr. Steven Kurtz, Accreditation Liaison Officer Ms. Tarie Zimmerman, Board Chair Dr. Jeff Wagnitz, Special Assistant to the President (Retired), Bates Technical College Jennifer White, Executive Director, Idaho State Board of Education 8060 165th Ave NE, Suite 200 | Redmond, WA 98052 | nwccu.org

What is the accreditation status of River Parishes Community College? River Parishes Community College remains an accredited member of the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges while on the sanction of Warning. Because SACSCOC is an institutional accreditor, this status applies to the all components of the institution—all programs, branch campuses, off-campus sites, and distance education programs as reported to SACSCOC. What does it mean to be placed on Warning? The sanction of Warning is imposed by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees in the early stages of institutional review following a determination of non-compliance with one or more of the Core Requirements and/or Standards of the Principles of Accreditation, the accreditation standards of SACSCOC; failure to make timely and significant progress toward correcting the deficiencies that led to the findings of non-compliance; or failure to comply with SACSCOC policies and procedures. The maximum total time during a monitoring period that an institution may be on sanction is two (2) years. In December 2026, River Parishes Community College will have completed its first year of monitoring. For additional information about sanctions, see the SACSCOC policy entitled Sanctions, Denial of Reaffirmation, and Removal from Membership. Why was River Parishes Community College placed on Warning? River Parishes Community College was placed on Warning because the SACSCOC Board of Trustees determined that the institution is in non-compliance with the Principles of Accreditation, namely, Core Requirement 8.1 (Student achievement), Standard 12.4 (Student complaints), and Standard 13.6 (Federal and state responsibilities) following review of a Referral Report. A Special Committee was not authorized to visit the institution. These standards expect an institution to: (1) Identify, evaluate, and publish goals and outcomes for student achievement appropriate to the institution’s mission; (2) (a) Publish appropriate and clear procedures for addressing written student complaints, (b) demonstrate that it follows the procedures when resolving them, and (c) maintain a record of student complaints that can be accessed upon request by SACSCOC; and that the institution (3) (a) is in compliance with its program responsibilities under Title IV of the most recent Higher Education Act as amended and (b) audits financial aid programs as required by federal and state regulations. actions.action_label: Accept the Report, Issue a Sanction of Probation on Recommendation 1 of the Fall 2025 Financial Resources Review
actions.notes: February 6, 2026 Dr. J. McNeil President The Seattle School of Theology and Psychology 1130 Rainier Ave South Seattle, WA 98144 Dear President McNeil: This letter serves as formal notification and official record of action taken by the Northwest Commission on Colleges and Universities (NWCCU) at its meeting on January 27-30, 2026, concerning the Fall 2025 Financial Resources Review of The Seattle School of Theology and Psychology. Accreditation Accept the Report, Issue a Sanction of Probation on Recommendation 1 of the Fall 2025 Financial Resources Review Recommendations Out of Compliance The Commission finds that the following Recommendations are areas where The Seattle School of Theology and Psychology is out of compliance with the NWCCU Standards for Accreditation. According to U.S. Department of Education Regulation 34 CFR 602.20 and Commission Policy, the Commission requires that The Seattle School of Theology and Psychology take appropriate action to ensure these Recommendations are addressed and resolved within the prescribed four-year period, no later than February 6, 2030. The Commission recommends that The Seattle School of Theology and Psychology: • Recommendation 1: Fall 2025 Financial Resources Review - Clearly demonstrate financial stability, including sufficient cash flow, to achieve and fulfill the institution’s mission. (2020 Standard(s) 2.E.1; ER 19) Probation Because of the significant financial issues at the institution, the Commission has issued the sanction of Probation, as stated in NWCCU’s Accreditation Actions Policy: NWCCU may place an institution on Probation and notes its Accreditation is in jeopardy when it does not appear to be in compliance with Standards for Accreditation, Eligibility Requirements, Policies, or applicable federal regulations, and there is evidence non- compliance is sufficiently serious, extensive, or substantial, that it raises concern about: quality of student learning experience; capacity to make appropriate improvements within a short period of time; capacity to sustain itself in the long term such that a teach-out plan is required; or information collected through monitoring activities suggests there are serious concerns related to student achievement, viability and capacity, or financial health. As a requirement of the Probation sanction, the institution will submit a Financial Resources Review (FRR) in Spring 2026 demonstrating the efforts the institution has made to come into compliance with identified NWCCU Standards for Accreditation (34 CFR 602.20(a)(2)). The institution must also develop teach-out plans, following the Procedures to Close a Program, Additional Location, Branch Campus, or Institution Policy. As noted in the Policy, “per Federal Regulation 34 CFR 602.24(c)(3), a teach-out plan must include (1) a list of currently enrolled students, (2) a list of academic 8060 165th Ave NE, Suite 200 | Redmond, WA 98052 | nwccu.org programs offered by the institution, and (3) the names of other institutions that offer similar programs and that could potentially enter into a teach-out agreement with the institution.” The teach out-plan, including these requirements, must be submitted to NWCCU within 60 days from receipt of this letter. In compliance with NWCCU’s Public Disclosures by Institutions Policy and per Federal Regulation 34 CFR 602.26(b)(e), the institution must notify current and prospective students and display an indicator of the sanction and non-compliance on its website within seven (7) calendar days of receipt of this letter. Future Evaluations • Financial Resources Review Spring 2026 o Recommendation 1: Fall 2025 Financial Resources Review • Year 6 - Standard 2 - Policies, Regulations, and Financial Review Fall 2026 • Year 7 - Evaluation of Institutional Effectiveness Fall 2027 o Recommendation 1: Fall 2023 Mid-Cycle Review o Recommendation 2: Fall 2023 Mid-Cycle Review NWCCU is committed to an accreditation process that adds value to institutions while contributing to public accountability, and we thank you for your continued support of this process. If you have questions about any of the information in this letter, please contact your staff liaison, Dr. Teresa Rivenes, at trivenes@nwccu.org. Sincerely, Selena M. actions.notes: August 11, 2025 Dr. Faimous Harrison President Wenatchee Valley College 1300 Fifth Street Wenatchee, WA 98801 Dear President Harrison: This letter serves as formal notification and official record of action taken by the Northwest Commission on Colleges and Universities (NWCCU) at its meeting on July 8-11, 2025, concerning the Spring 2025 Special Report and the Spring 2025 Policies, Regulations, and Financial Review of Wenatchee Valley College. Accreditation Accept the Special Report, Do Not Accept the Policies, Regulations, and Financial Review. The institution must undergo comprehensive Evaluation of Institutional Effectiveness visit in Spring 2026 to address Standards 1 and 2. Issue Sanction of Warning on Recommendations 1 and 2 Spring 2025 Special Report. Recommendations Out of Compliance The Commission finds that the following Recommendations are areas where Wenatchee Valley College is out of compliance with the NWCCU Standards for Accreditation. According to U.S. Department of Education Regulation 34 CFR 602.20 and Commission Policy, the Commission requires that Wenatchee Valley College take appropriate action to ensure these Recommendations are addressed and resolved within the prescribed four-year period, by no later than August 2029. The Commission recommends that Wenatchee Valley College: • Recommendation 1: Spring 2025 Special Report - All administrators and supervisors receive ongoing quality training on ethical management, ethical operations, transparent communication, and the respectful, consistent, fair, timely, and ethical handling of complaints and grievances. (2020 Standard(s) 2.D.2; ER 7; ER 8) • Recommendation 2: Spring 2025 Special Report - Employees receive ongoing quality training on effective workplace communication, preventing bullying, harassment, and discrimination, and how the complaint and grievance process works (including confidentiality). (2020 Standard(s) 2.D.2; ER 7; ER 8) Future Evaluations • Year 7 - Evaluation of Institutional Effectiveness Spring 2026 o Recommendation 1: Spring 2025 Special Report o Recommendation 2: Spring 2025 Special Report NWCCU is committed to an accreditation process that adds value to institutions while contributing to public accountability, and we thank you for your continued support of this process. If you have questions about any of the information in this letter, please contact your staff liaison, Dr. Teresa Rivenes, at trivenes@nwccu.org. Sincerely, Selena M.
